message:
feat(storage): tick 169 WriteCommandSender::send_with_cx closes root-level orphan (ft-xbnl0.2.3)

Twentieth post-storage tick. Found and plugged the largest single
orphan-cx hole in the codebase via the call-graph scan follow-up
to tick 168. Tick's impact is architectural: it unblocks
progressive migration of the full storage write surface to
cancellation-interruptible backpressure.

Problem: `WriteCommandSender::send` (the inner mpsc-wrap used by
every storage write) reserves with `crate::cx::for_request()` on
the asupersync path. This orphan cx severs the cancellation chain
from every storage `_with_cx` caller in the tree (~60+ methods).
The pre-flight `cx.checkpoint()` in each sibling catches cancel
before the enqueue, but if the writer queue is backpressured, the
actual reserve wait ignores the parent cx.

New sibling:
- WriteCommandSender::send_with_cx — threads caller cx into
  `self.inner.send(cx, command)`. Legacy `send` preserved for
  ambient-cx callers (which include ~50+ legacy `foo(...)` paired
  writes that don't have cx in scope).

Call-site migrations (tick 136 event-annotation cluster, 6
methods — hot-path writes, coherent single-cluster scope):
- mark_event_handled_with_cx
- set_event_triage_state_with_cx
- set_event_note_with_cx
- add_event_label_with_cx
- remove_event_label_with_cx
- add_event_mute_with_cx

Migration pattern: inlined the write-command construction + send
directly (rather than delegating to the legacy), so the `cx` is
in scope for the `send_with_cx` call. The legacy delegate path
wouldn't have worked — the legacy fn constructs and sends in one
unit.

Verified via fork bypass: 496/496 storage:: tests pass. The
`storage_tick136_event_annotation_cluster_roundtrip` regression
gate pins the byte-parity behavior of the 6 migrated methods.

Future ticks can progressively migrate the remaining ~50+
`_with_cx` storage write methods to the same pattern. This tick
lands the infrastructure + a representative cluster to prove the
pattern.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
